lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 17 - ConvNets and TreeRNNs for NLP

unit.name: ConvNets for language: intuition and core operations
unit.description: Introduces 1D convolutions over word sequences and the main pooling and receptive-field choices used in text CNNs.
unit.summary: After brief course housekeeping, the lecture frames CNNs as an alternative to sequential sentence modeling: instead of building a prefix representation left-to-right, a text CNN computes local n-gram features over word vectors [ts=24s][ts=297s]. The instructor maps the classic 2D vision convolution idea to 1D language by sliding a trigram-sized filter over adjacent word embeddings, taking a dot product, adding bias, and applying a nonlinearity to get features for each window [ts=383s][ts=530s]. He then explains padding for same-length or wider outputs, using multiple filters to produce richer position-wise representations, and pooling as the main way to summarize activations across the sentence [ts=586s][ts=629s][ts=678s]. Max pooling is motivated as feature detection anywhere in the text, average pooling as an alternative summary statistic, and the section closes with further design knobs including stride, local max pooling, k-max pooling, and dilation for larger receptive fields [ts=743s][ts=910s][ts=1026s].

key_points.text: Text CNNs build representations for local n-grams rather than only left-to-right prefixes.
A 1D convolution computes a filter-window dot product over an n-gram, then adds bias and applies a nonlinearity.
Zero padding preserves sequence length, and wider padding can increase output length.
Using multiple filters yields a new vector representation at each position instead of a single scalar feature.
Max pooling is useful when a filter acts like a feature detector that only needs to fire strongly somewhere in the text.
Stride, local max pooling, k-max pooling, and dilation change overlap and receptive field in text convolutions.

unit_summary_for_generation: After brief course housekeeping, the lecture frames CNNs as an alternative to sequential sentence modeling: instead of building a prefix representation left-to-right, a text CNN computes local n-gram features over word vectors [ts=24s][ts=297s]. The instructor maps the classic 2D vision convolution idea to 1D language by sliding a trigram-sized filter over adjacent word embeddings, taking a dot product, adding bias, and applying a nonlinearity to get features for each window [ts=383s][ts=530s]. He then explains padding for same-length or wider outputs, using multiple filters to produce richer position-wise representations, and pooling as the main way to summarize activations across the sentence [ts=586s][ts=629s][ts=678s]. Max pooling is motivated as feature detection anywhere in the text, average pooling as an alternative summary statistic, and the section closes with further design knobs including stride, local max pooling, k-max pooling, and dilation for larger receptive fields [ts=743s][ts=910s][ts=1026s].

kp_catalog.name: Text CNN framing
kp_catalog.description: CNNs model language with local receptive fields over token sequences instead of sequential state updates.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Text CNN framing captures the core idea that CNNs model language with local receptive fields over token sequences instead of sequential state updates.

kp_catalog.name: 1D convolution over n-grams
kp_catalog.description: A text convolution slides a learned filter over adjacent word-vector windows to compute local features.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because 1D convolution over n-grams provides prerequisite machinery that later units build on directly.

kp_catalog.name: Pooling strategies in text CNNs
kp_catalog.description: Max, average, local, and k-max pooling determine how local activations are summarized across a sentence.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Pooling strategies in text CNNs captures the core idea that Max, average, local, and k-max pooling determine how local activations are summarized across a sentence.

kp_catalog.name: Convolution hyperparameters and tensor shapes
kp_catalog.description: Understanding filter count, kernel size, stride, padding, and the resulting input/output tensor shapes for convolution layers.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because convolution hyperparameters and tensor shapes clarifies understanding filter count, kernel size, stride, padding, and the resulting input/output tensor shapes for convolution layers, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
